title: 1.1.0 - Introduction | Drawing Machines 101

segments.text: I really like pen plotters because we're
using code to control a machine that's
holding a pen and drawing on paper and
that feels and is very precise, but at
the same time, things don't always go to
plan. And that's where the interesting
stuff often starts to happen. Hi, I'm
Dan Cat and this is Drawing Machines
101. Quick note before we start. It's
autumn at the moment when I'm recording
this video. And there's no heating in
the studio at all, which means as the
videos go on. We'll be recording through
winter. And I find it important to say I
am not getting fatter during these
videos, I'm just putting more more
layers on underneath this hoodie here.
So, by the end of it, I'm going to be
like this.
>> [music]
>> Well, hey, as this is the introduction,
let's keep it nice and short and just
bash through what this video is going to
cover. But first things first, if you go
to penplotter.com, this is where all
these videos will live. Either this is
the first one and we've only just
started or we're halfway through or
they're all there. [music] But you'll
find all the resources, links, files,
everything you need will be over there.
And that will show you what we're going
to be covering as well. The second thing
I want to say is this is for two types
of people. The first type of people are
people who already code. Maybe you
already do creative coding. Maybe you're
an engineer and you're [music] trying to
figure out how to talk to these machines
cuz it's kind of cool and it is. And
[music] we'll go through all of that.
The second type of person is someone
who's never coded before and we'll talk
more about that in just a moment. So,
first what we're going to do is we're
going to talk about these [music]
drawing machines. So, here I have an old
Axidraw, which is one of the first
machines that I had. Here is a much
bigger machine. This is the art frame.
[music] And on here we've got the iDraw
Unitech. And then we've got a teeny tiny
A5. No, this is an A6 plotter. [music]
And then we also have the new Next Draw
over here. So these are the machines
we're going to be using. And then we're
going to be talking about pens. [music]
More pens. More pens. Fountain pens. So
much ink. And then paper as well. Big
paper, small paper, notepads, weird
paper, all sorts of things that you can
plot on as well. So we're going to cover
all of that. And then if you already
know how to code, that will probably be
enough to get you going. Drawing
machines, how to talk to them, pens,
paper, and some knowledge that I have
for you. However, if you're interested
in getting started right from scratch
and know nothing about coding,
[music]
I am writing code. Writing code is very
easy and straightforward. But actually,
in all seriousness, it isn't that hard.
So, what we're going to be doing after
the introduction to the pen [music]
plotter is then move into the coding
part of the whole thing. And we're going
to start off real basic and then work
our way up. I think a lot of creative
coding courses out there, which are all
fantastic, start with this is what
programming and coding is and this is
how we make art with it. I'm an artist
and you're probably an artist. We're
going to come from an artist point of
view where we're going to go what are we
trying to do? What's the art we're going
to try to make? and then how do I use
code to make that art? In a later video,
we'll talk about all the setup you need
for that. But just so you know, it
doesn't matter what coding language
you're going to use. [music] It can be
Python or NodeJS or JavaScript. That's
the one that we're going to do. Um,
processing. You can use all of them.
You'll see when we start that it doesn't
actually matter what [music] language.
It's all fine. Let's go over there. Just
one more thing before we move on. You
don't even need to have a drawing
machine. So, here we have a printer and
we're printing out one of the designs
that we're going to be doing.
And as you can see, here's the pen
plotted version. Here's the printed
version. So, if you're on the fence
about getting one of these drawing
machines, you can still follow along,
print the things out, decide if you like
it or not, and then work out what to do
next. All right, I think that's right.
So, once again, I am Daniel Cat. I'm
based here in my art studio in Shrewsby
in the UK. And this is a a proper
working art studio. This isn't like a
YouTube studio or something like that.
We have artists up above and there are
artists down below. So, if you hear
background noise going on, banging stuff
like that, I can only apologize. Um,
this place is impossible to soundproof.
So, you'll have to forgive me on that
front. I'm in here nearly every day
working on all these machines. All these
joy machines are generally constantly
going. And over the next few months, I'm
[music] going to be making this whole
series of not tutorial videos, just how
to do this stuff, cuz I really love
making this stuff work. [music]
Anyway, let's get on to the next video,
which is what is a drawing machine? That
will probably be over here somewhere or
maybe over here. It depends which side I
put it. Okay, let's get on
